metadata.title: Build a Budget Stress-Tester with Claude: See the Cascade Before the Money Moves

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The raise-plus-car-payment scenario rewired how I think, because a $250 raise combined with a $300 fixed payment nets negative fifty a month. I want to know how fragile my own budget is to permanent commitments. Help me: (1) rebuild budget_stress.py to model a raise that has a 30 percent chance of not materializing, (2) show the deficit distribution instead of a single number, (3) project the ten-year compound cost of treating that uncertain raise as if it were guaranteed.

props.command: The raise-plus-car-payment scenario rewired how I think, because a $250 raise combined with a $300 fixed payment nets negative fifty a month. I want to know how fragile my own budget is to permanent commitments. Help me: (1) rebuild budget_stress.py to model a raise that has a 30 percent chance of not materializing, (2) show the deficit distribution instead of a single number, (3) project the ten-year compound cost of treating that uncertain raise as if it were guaranteed.
props.segment: Build a Budget Stress-Tester with Claude:

beats.narration_text: Elan walked into the store with a budget and walked out $2000 in debt. Each individual decision was defensible. The cascade only became visible after it started.

The budget is sensitive to small choices — this is the feature that makes it useful. The sensitivity means you can see the cascade before it starts. A zero-based budget plus a stress test — increase variable expenses by 20 percent — makes the cascade visible in the terminal before it happens in the bank account.
Ask Claude to write budget_stress.py with zero-based budget, 20 percent stress test, and 10-year compound growth.

props.command: claude "Write budget_stress.py that: (1) takes income=2500, savings_amount=360, expenses as (category, amount, type) tuples; (2) prints zero-based budget table; (3) stress-tests by increasing all variable expenses by 20%; (4) shows deficit and ranked cut list (variable first, then fixed); (5) prints 10-year compound growth of savings at 6% annually. Demo: [('Housing',750,'fixed'),('Car+Insurance',450,'fixed'),('Groceries',400,'variable'),('Phone',120,'variable'),('Gas',200,'variable'),('Medical',120,'fixed'),('Restaurants',100,'variable'),('Entertainment',60,'variable')]."

beats.narration_text: The stress-test logic: variable expense identification, 20 percent increase calculation, deficit computation, ranked cut list, compound interest formula.

Animated bar chart showing the zero-based budget before stress test: 8 expense bars with income ceiling. Then stress test: variable expense bars extend upward 20 percent; deficit zone appears in crimson. Total income: $2500. Pre-stress expenses plus savings: $2500, balanced. Post-stress expenses plus savings: $2690. Deficit: $190.
Add second stress-test: 10 percent raise plus $300 per month new car payment.

Animated comparison: base budget bar versus raise scenario. Raise adds $250 per month income. New car payment adds $300 per month fixed cost. Net effect: minus $50 per month deficit. The raise looks better than it is — because the fixed expense is permanent while raises are not guaranteed.

The stress-tester makes the cascade visible before the money moves. The zero-based constraint — every dollar assigned — prevents the quiet overflow, the money that just disappears. The 10-year compound projection is the other side of the ledger: $360 per month for 10 years at 6 percent annual grows to approximately $58,000. The math for saving is as visible as the math for spending.
Next: research the three critical thinking modes — which tool for which academic problem.

beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
